narrative: I'm sorry to share this tough news, but the law school's admission model predicted an 87% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1—much lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class—as well as your Year 3 decile also at 1, compared to averages in the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 29 falls well below the dataset average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 is lower than the typical 3.2. While your family income is in the middle bracket, which is common, your background as a Black female pursuing full-time studies aligns with smaller groups in the data, where White and male full-time students dominate. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your low Year 1 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction strongly toward expecting bar failure. Next in importance was your LSAT score, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, each contributing to the failure outlook in that order. Your middle family income and racial background had moderate influence toward the same direction, with your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status playing smaller but still negative roles. Overall, these factors combined to make your profile stand out as riskier for bar passage compared to typical admitted students who have stronger early grades, higher test scores, and GPAs. It's not a reflection of your potential, but rather how these metrics stacked up against patterns in past bar takers. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your current school performance to climb those decile rankings, as that's the top factor you can influence right now. Retaking the LSAT to aim above 35 could make a big difference too, since it's the second-biggest driver. Building a stronger GPA through additional coursework or post-undergrad achievements might help offset the undergrad mark, and seeking bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students could address other patterns in the data. With targeted effort on these, you could shift the model's view in your favor for future applications. Keep pushing—you've got this.
